headline: Medical Tourism Hit RM2.72B in 2024; 2030 Target RM12B, Public Specialist Shortages Persist
context: MHTC reported RM2.72 billion in 2024 medical-tourism revenue from 1.6 million healthcare travellers (+14% year-on-year). The 2025 target was RM3 billion; early-2025 figures showed 1.84 million travellers. The 2030 target is RM12 billion.

cards.big: Medical tourism earned RM2.72 billion in 2024, targeting RM12 billion by 2030, while public hospitals continue to face specialist shortages.
cards.sub: MHTC: 1.6 million healthcare travellers in 2024. Early-2025 figures already at 1.84 million. The RM12B 2030 target is about 4× the 2024 actual.

cards.big: MHTC shortlisted several major hospitals for the Flagship Medical Tourism Hospital (FMTH) programme; MOH supports public hospitals separately.
cards.sub: As of July 2025, recognised finalists included Institut Jantung Negara, Island Hospital Penang, Mahkota Medical Centre and Subang Jaya Medical Centre.

cards.big: Scaling medical tourism 4× by 2030 raises a workforce-planning question: where the additional specialist capacity will come from.
cards.sub: If export growth draws on scarce specialist capacity, public-sector queues face pressure. The trade-off is a policy question, not a settled outcome.

cards.big: Some jurisdictions use cross-subsidy mechanisms. India's Charitable Hospital obligations reserve beds for low-income local patients.
cards.sub: Malaysian private-healthcare regulations contain no comparable mandatory reserved-beds-for-locals requirement in the sources reviewed.

cards.big: The growth story is real. The open structural question is whether specialist supply expands fast enough that public-sector waits do not lengthen as exports rise.
Like a restaurant that serves tourists a five-course meal while the kitchen staff eat leftovers: same building, different service tiers.
cards.sub: Medical-tourism growth may create workforce trade-offs if it draws on scarce specialist capacity faster than training pipelines refill it.

cards.big: Medical tourism is a legitimate export. The reform proposal: expand specialist supply alongside it, and consider local cross-subsidy at high-volume hospitals.
